audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Pharaoh audience, Goshen/Rameses settlement, Jacob blessing Pharaoh, Joseph's famine administration, land transfer, priest exception, Israel in Goshen, and Jacob's burial oath.

verses.restored: Joseph came and told Pharaoh, My father and my brothers, their flocks, herds, and all they have have come from Canaan, and look, they are in Goshen.
From among his brothers he took five men and presented them before Pharaoh.
Pharaoh said to his brothers, What is your work? They said to Pharaoh, Your servants are shepherds, both we and our fathers.
They said to Pharaoh, We have come to sojourn in the land, for there is no pasture for your servants’ flocks because the famine is heavy in Canaan. Now please let your servants dwell in Goshen.
Pharaoh said to Joseph, Your father and your brothers have come to you.
The land of Egypt is before you. Settle your father and brothers in the best of the land; let them dwell in Goshen. If you know capable men among them, appoint them over my livestock.
Joseph brought Jacob his father and stood him before Pharaoh, and Jacob blessed Pharaoh.
Pharaoh said to Jacob, How many are the days of the years of your life?

Jacob said to Pharaoh, The days of the years of my sojournings are one hundred thirty years; few and evil have been the days of the years of my life, and they have not reached the days of my fathers in the days of their sojournings.
Jacob blessed Pharaoh and went out from Pharaoh’s presence.
Joseph settled his father and brothers and gave them a possession in Egypt, in the best of the land, in the land of Rameses, as Pharaoh had commanded.
Joseph sustained his father, his brothers, and all his father’s house with bread according to the little ones.
There was no bread in all the land, for the famine was very heavy; the land of Egypt and the land of Canaan languished because of the famine.
Joseph gathered all the silver found in Egypt and Canaan for the grain they were buying, and Joseph brought the silver into Pharaoh’s house.
When the silver was finished from Egypt and Canaan, all Egypt came to Joseph, saying, Give us bread. Why should we die before you? For the silver is gone.
Joseph said, Give your livestock, and I will give you bread for your livestock if the silver is gone.
They brought their livestock to Joseph, and Joseph gave them bread for horses, flocks, herds, and donkeys; he guided them with bread that year for all their livestock.

When that year ended, they came to him the second year and said, We will not hide from my lord that the silver is finished and the livestock belongs to my lord; nothing remains before my lord except our bodies and our ground.

Why should we die before your eyes, both we and our ground? Buy us and our ground for bread, and we and our ground will be servants to Pharaoh. Give seed so we may live and not die, and the ground not be desolate.
Joseph bought all the ground of Egypt for Pharaoh, for each Egyptian sold his field because the famine was strong upon them; the land became Pharaoh’s.
As for the people, he moved them to the cities, from one end of Egypt’s border to the other.
Only the ground of the priests he did not buy, for the priests had an allotment from Pharaoh and ate their allotment that Pharaoh gave them; therefore they did not sell their ground.
Joseph said to the people, Look, I have bought you and your ground today for Pharaoh. Here is seed for you, and you shall sow the ground.
At the harvest you shall give a fifth to Pharaoh, and four parts shall be yours for seed of the field, for your food, for those in your houses, and for your little ones to eat.
They said, You have kept us alive; let us find favor in my lord’s eyes, and we will be servants to Pharaoh.
Joseph made it a statute concerning the ground of Egypt to this day, that Pharaoh should have the fifth; only the ground of the priests alone did not become Pharaoh’s.
Israel dwelled in the land of Egypt, in the land of Goshen; they acquired holdings in it, were fruitful, and multiplied greatly.
Jacob lived in the land of Egypt seventeen years, and the days of Jacob, the years of his life, were one hundred forty-seven years.

When the days of Israel drew near to die, he called Joseph his son and said, If now I have found favor in your eyes, place your hand under my thigh and do covenant-kindness and truth with me: please do not bury me in Egypt.
When I lie down with my fathers, carry me out of Egypt and bury me in their burial place. He said, I will do according to your word.
He said, Swear to me. He swore to him. Then Israel bowed at the head of the bed.
